pairs.input: We plan to store user passwords in plain text in the database for simplicity.

observations: Storing passwords in plain text is a critical security vulnerability
This violates OWASP security guidelines and most compliance frameworks
A database breach would expose all user credentials immediately
suggestions: Use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2id for password hashing
Implement salting to prevent rainbow table attacks
Consider adding rate limiting on authentication endpoints
Plan for password rotation and breach notification procedures

pairs.input: Our deployment process involves manually copying files to the production server via FTP.

observations: Manual deployments are error-prone and difficult to reproduce
FTP transmits credentials and files in plain text
No rollback mechanism exists for failed deployments
No audit trail of what was deployed and when
suggestions: Implement a CI/CD pipeline with automated builds and deployments
Use SFTP or SCP at minimum; prefer container-based deployment
Add automated smoke tests that run after each deployment
Implement blue-green or canary deployment for zero-downtime updates

pairs.input: We're building a real-time chat application using HTTP polling every 500ms.

observations: Polling at 500ms creates unnecessary server load and network traffic
Messages will have up to 500ms delivery latency
This approach does not scale well with increasing user count
Battery and bandwidth impact on mobile clients will be significant
suggestions: Use WebSockets for bidirectional real-time communication
Consider Server-Sent Events (SSE) if only server-to-client updates are needed
Implement connection heartbeats and automatic reconnection
Add message acknowledgment to handle delivery guarantees

pairs.input: Our monolithic application handles 10,000 requests per second and we want to break it into microservices.

observations: 10,000 RPS suggests the monolith is performing adequately
Microservices introduce network latency, operational complexity, and distributed system challenges
The migration will require significant investment in infrastructure and tooling
Team expertise in distributed systems should be evaluated
suggestions: Identify specific pain points the monolith creates before migrating
Consider a modular monolith as an intermediate step
Extract only the components with clear bounded contexts first
Invest in observability tooling before splitting services

pairs.input: We want to use a single global Redux store for all application state including form inputs and API cache.

observations: Storing all state in one Redux store leads to unnecessary re-renders
Form state is inherently local and ephemeral
API cache has different invalidation needs than UI state
The store will become increasingly difficult to debug as it grows
suggestions: Use local component state or a form library for form inputs
Use React Query or SWR for server state and API caching
Reserve Redux for truly global, client-side application state
Consider Zustand or Jotai for simpler global state needs
